hook: You already know how to draw a graph by sketching vertices and edges on paper, and you can identify neighbors by eye. But imagine you need to write a program that finds the shortest path in a network of a million routers — you can't hand a drawing to a computer. How do we translate the visual structure of a graph into a precise data format that an algorithm can process? That's exactly the gap this lesson closes.
concept: A graph representation is a formal data structure that encodes the vertices and edges of a graph $G = (V, E)$ in a way a computer (or mathematician) can manipulate systematically. The two most common representations are the adjacency matrix, an $n \times n$ array $A$ where $A[i][j] = 1$ if edge $(i,j) \in E$ and $0$ otherwise, and the adjacency list, a collection of $n$ lists where the list for vertex $i$ contains every neighbor of $i$.
anatomy: Both representations share the same underlying graph with $n = |V|$ vertices and $m = |E|$ edges, but package that information differently. In the adjacency matrix $A$, the row index $i$ and column index $j$ each represent a vertex, and the entry $A[i][j]$ is the relationship flag (1 for adjacent, 0 for not). In the adjacency list, each vertex $v_i$ owns a list $L_i$ of neighbor vertices; the degree of $v_i$ equals $|L_i|$. For an undirected graph both structures are symmetric: $A[i][j] = A[j][i]$ and $v_j \in L_i \Leftrightarrow v_i \in L_j$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by clearly writing out the vertex set and edge set of our example graph. Having every edge listed explicitly is the foundation for both representations — we will read from this list to fill in the matrix and the lists, so getting this right first prevents downstream errors.

To build the adjacency matrix, label rows and columns $1$ through $4$, then set $A[i][j] = 1$ exactly when $(i,j) \in E$. For instance, edge $(1,2)$ sets both $A[1][2]$ and $A[2][1]$ to $1$ because the graph is undirected, which is why the matrix is symmetric about the main diagonal.

For the adjacency list, we scan the edge set and, for each edge $(i,j)$, append $j$ to list $L_i$ and $i$ to list $L_j$. Vertex 1 appears in edges $(1,2)$ and $(1,3)$, so its list is $[2,3]$; you can verify each list by counting which edges touch that vertex.

Now we compare storage costs. The matrix always allocates $n^2$ cells regardless of how many edges exist, while the adjacency list stores exactly $n$ list headers plus $2m$ neighbor references for an undirected graph. For sparse graphs (few edges relative to $n^2$), the list representation is far more memory-efficient.

partial_example.description_latex: We have a small path graph on three vertices. Our goal is to build both its adjacency matrix and its adjacency list. Start by noting $n = 3$ and $m = 2$, which tells us the matrix will be $3 \times 3$ and each adjacency list will have at most 2 entries.

Here is the completed adjacency matrix with rows and columns ordered $A, B, C$. Notice that $A[B][B] = 0$ because there are no self-loops, and the matrix is symmetric. Use this matrix to check your adjacency list in the next step.

Now write out the adjacency list for each vertex: $L_A$, $L_B$, and $L_C$. For each vertex, list every neighbor by scanning the edges. Which vertex has degree 2, and how does that show up in its list?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Write the adjacency matrix for } G \text{ with } V=\{1,2,3\},\; E=\{(1,2),(2,3)\}.
\text{Given adjacency matrix } A = \begin{pmatrix} 0&1&1\\1&0&0\\1&0&0 \end{pmatrix}, \text{ list the edges of } G.
\text{Write the adjacency list for } G \text{ with } V=\{1,2,3,4\},\; E=\{(1,2),(1,4),(2,3),(3,4)\}.

\text{Graph } G \text{ has } n=5 \text{ vertices and } m=3 \text{ edges (undirected, no self-loops). How many 1-entries appear in its adjacency matrix, and what is the total number of neighbor references stored across all adjacency lists?}

\text{A social network has 4 users. User 1 follows 2 and 3; User 2 follows 4; User 3 follows 4. Model this as a directed graph and write its adjacency matrix } A \text{, where } A[i][j]=1 \text{ means } i \text{ follows } j.

common_mistakes: Students fill in only half the adjacency matrix for an undirected graph — they set $A[i][j]=1$ but forget to also set $A[j][i]=1$. Remember: an undirected edge $(i,j)$ means both directions are valid, so the matrix must be symmetric; always update both entries simultaneously.

When reading an adjacency list, students count the number of lists instead of the total entries to find the number of edges, leading to $m = n$ instead of $m = (\sum |L_i|)/2$. The correct rule is that each undirected edge appears twice across all lists, so divide the total neighbor-reference count by 2.

Students index the adjacency matrix starting at row/column 0 in code but at 1 in their written work, causing off-by-one mismatches when checking whether $A[i][j]$ matches the edge list. Always agree on a single indexing convention before building any representation and stick to it throughout.
untested_variants: Weighted graphs, where $A[i][j]$ stores the edge weight (e.g., distance or capacity) rather than a binary 0/1 flag, and how the adjacency list stores (neighbor, weight) pairs.
Converting between representations in the reverse direction: given an adjacency list, reconstructing the full adjacency matrix for a graph with 5 or more vertices.
Incidence matrices, where rows represent vertices and columns represent edges, as a third representation not covered in the practice problems.

Analyzing time complexity differences between the two representations for specific operations such as checking whether edge $(u,v)$ exists ($O(1)$ for matrix vs. $O(\deg(u))$ for list) or iterating over all neighbors of a vertex.
